title-group.article-title: Is long-term disruption of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system associated with long COVID? A retrospective cohort study among healthcare workers

contrib-group.aff: 2https://ror.org/03kgsv495grid.22098.310000 0004 1937 0503The Azrieli faculty of medicine, Bar Ilan University, Safed, Israel
3https://ror.org/03qryx823grid.6451.60000000121102151Ruth & Bruce Rappaport Faculty of Medicine, Technion-IIT, Haifa, Israel
4https://ror.org/01fm87m50grid.413731.30000 0000 9950 8111Department of Laboratory Medicine, Rambam Health Care Campus, Haifa, Israel

license.license-p: Open Access This article is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivatives 4.0 International License, which permits any non-commercial use, sharing, distribution and reproduction in any medium or format, as long as you give appropriate credit to the original author(s) and the source, provide a link to the Creative Commons licence, and indicate if you modified the licensed material. You do not have permission under this licence to share adapted material derived from this article or parts of it. The images or other third party material in this article are included in the article’s Creative Commons licence, unless indicated otherwise in a credit line to the material. If material is not included in the article’s Creative Commons licence and your intended use is not permitted by statutory regulation or exceeds the permitted use, you will need to obtain permission directly from the copyright holder. To view a copy of this licence, visit http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Angiotensin converting enzyme 2 (ACE2), a component of the renin-angiotensin aldosterone system (RAAS), is the main receptor for SARS-CoV-2 entry into human cells. The occurrence of long-term symptoms post-COVID 19 infection (Long COVID, LC) is an important public health issue with an unclear etiological mechanism. We aimed to determine whether LC was associated with long-term RAAS disruption.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: We recruited a cohort of healthcare workers (HCWs) from Ziv Medical Center in Safed, Israel, who were uninfected and unvaccinated at baseline, and who later became infected with SARS-CoV-2. We measured serum circulating levels of four RAAS components (ACE, ACE2, Ang1-7 and AngII) using commercially available ELISA assays at three time points, using serum samples regularly collected from consenting hospital workers during the COVID-19 pandemic: pre-infection, 3–6 months post-infection, and a year post-infection. Post-serum collection we determined LC status using an online survey based on self-reported, LC-compatible symptoms not explained by alternative diagnoses. We excluded participants with conditions or medications interfering with the RAAS (e.g. hypertension, chronic kidney disease, antihypertensives, antidiuretics). At each time point we compared the levels of each of the four RAAS components between those infected and reporting LC and those infected not reporting LC using Mann Whitney U tests and Wilcoxon signed-rank tests, corrected for multiple testing.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: We included 38 LC positive and 38 LC negative participants. Age/gender distribution was similar in both groups. No statistically significant differences in any of the four RAAS markers were observed between LC cases and controls at either 3–6 months or 12 months post-infection in our study sample.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: No evidence was detected within the limits of the study design and sample size to conclude that long-term disruption of RAAS is a significant contributor to LC pathophysiology.

sec.title: Clinical trial
sec.p: Not applicable.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p: The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1186/s12879-026-12527-z.

custom-meta.meta-value: © BioMed Central Ltd., part of Springer Nature 2026

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) is an infectious respiratory illness caused by infection with the SARS-CoV-2 virus, and is characterized by varied clinical outcomes, ranging from asymptomatic cases to severe respiratory failure, multi-organ dysfunction, and death [1]. Although most cases are self-limiting, a substantial proportion of patients go on to develop a range of long-term or recurring symptoms, known as post-acute sequelae of COVID-19 (PASC) or “long COVID” (LC), which continue for months or years after the acute phase of COVID-19 infection [2]. As of August 2024, the prevalence of LC is estimated to be about 6–7% of adults [3]. Symptoms of LC vary widely from person to person and impact multiple organ systems, including the respiratory, cardiovascular, neurological, and gastrointestinal systems [4], with a sometimes profound and lasting impact on quality of life and well-being [5]. The most frequently reported symptoms of LC include fatigue, headache, attention deficit, hair loss, and upper respiratory tract complaints [6]. Known risk factors for developing LC include female sex; older age; pre-existing health conditions, such as obesity, cardiovascular disease, chronic lung disease, kidney disease, hypertension, and diabetes mellitus [7]; and a more severe initial acute COVID-19 infection [8].

sec.title: Possible mechanisms of long COVID
sec.p: While the pathophysiological mechanisms of LC are still being understood, several mechanistic pathways have been proposed for its emergence. These include viral persistence of the SARS-CoV-2 virus, immune dysregulation, mitochondrial dysfunction, complement dysregulation, endothelial and prothrombotic inflammation, and microbiome dysbiosis [3]. The diverse and complex symptomatology of LC most likely cannot be explained by a single mechanistic pathway. Instead, it likely arises from the interplay of multiple pathological pathways, each characterized by distinct biological mechanisms, risk factors, and disease manifestations. These pathways are shaped by a range of factors, including prior environmental exposures, genetic predisposition, age, sex, viral characteristics, immune responses, and medical treatments, which may trigger the pathophysiological mechanisms that produce LC phenotypes [3].

sec.title: COVID-19 and the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system
sec.p: In the acute phase of COVID-19, the SARS-CoV-2 virus infects cells by binding to the angiotensin-converting enzyme 2 (ACE2) receptor, a key regulator of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (RAAS), which controls cardiovascular, pulmonary, renal, immune, and gut microbiome inflammatory functions [9]. Consequently, the role of ACE2 and the RAAS in the pathogenesis of COVID-19 is of particular interest. ACE2 plays a critical role in converting the octapeptide angiotensin II (Ang II) to angiotensin (1–7) [10]. When Ang II binds to the angiotensin type 1 receptor (AT1R), it triggers a pro-inflammatory response. When Ang (1–7) binds to the Mas receptor (MasR), it reduces inflammation [10] and confers anti-fibrotic effects [11, 12]. The RAAS therefore operates via two main pathways: the pro-inflammatory ACE/Ang II/AT1R axis and the anti-inflammatory ACE2/Ang-(1–7)/AT2R axis, with ACE2 maintaining homeostasis [9].

The overactivation of the pro-inflammatory RAAS pathway has been identified as a critical factor in COVID-19 symptomatology [13]. When SARS-CoV-2 binds to ACE2, it reduces ACE2 activity [10]. Downregulation of ACE2 leads to an excess of Ang II, which overactivates the AT1 receptor, causing significant immune dysregulation and increased inflammation during SARS-CoV-2 infection [9]. It has been suggested that the overactivation of the immune system by the RAAS may contribute to both acute and LC symptoms [10]. While a link between RAAS overactivation and LC has been hypothesized [9], the underlying mechanisms have yet to be elucidated. One suggestion is that after SARS-CoV-2 infection, some patients may develop autoantibodies against ACE2 for an extended period of time, which inhibits ACE2 activity and triggers the immune activation seen in LC [10]. By disrupting the RAAS, decreased ACE2 activity can exacerbate the pro-inflammatory and pro-fibrotic effects of Ang II, potentially contributing to LC symptoms [10]. Another possibility is that persistent shedding of ACE2, which is associated with decreased activity of membrane-bound ACE2 [14] could contribute to a reduction in ACE2 enzymatic activity, resulting in prolonged immune activation and LC symptoms [10].

Both ACE and ACE2 can be shed from cell membranes, generating soluble ACE (sACE) and soluble ACE2 (sACE2), which retain enzymatic activity [15, 16]. Elevated levels of sACE2 have been observed in hospital-treated COVID-19 patients [17]. While the systemic RAAS regulates blood pressure, fluid balance, and electrolyte levels across the body, many organs have their own localized RAAS systems to manage organ-specific functions such as tissue repair and inflammation [18]. Additionally, viral persistence or immune activation may interfere with local RAAS systems, potentially leading to organ-specific inflammation and regional tissue damage.

sec.title: Rationale
sec.p: While the theoretical role of the RAAS in LC pathogenesis has been hypothesized, there are relatively few studies testing this hypothesis. A few studies have shown that long-term activation of certain RAAS pathways, like the ACE/Ang II/AT1R axis, could lead to systemic inflammation and tissue damage, which might explain some of the chronic symptoms observed in LC patients [13, 19]. In this study, we measured the serum levels of key RAAS components (ACE, ACE2, AngII, and Ang 1–7) in individuals before and after SARS-CoV-2 infection. As some of these individuals later reported LC compatible symptoms, we aimed to determine whether we could detect long-term (i.e. after the initial acute episode had resolved) differences in RAAS component levels between those with and without LC. Such information may help hypothesize a contributing mechanism for LC and inform research into therapeutic options for an emerging chronic health condition affecting tens of millions of individuals globally.

sec.title: Participants and specimen collection
sec.p: Participants were recruited from a cohort of approximately 1,000 healthcare workers (HCW) in Ziv Medical Center (ZMC) in Safed, Israel, the details of which are described elsewhere [20]. In brief, HCW were recruited in December 2020 before they received the first dose of the BNT162b2 COVID-19 vaccine. Blood specimens of enrolled participants were collected every 2–4 months until September 2022. Soluble RAAS components were measured each time, using assays described below. HCWs were regularly tested by PCR to determine infection status, with a low clinical threshold for testing for SARS-CoV-2 infection.

In this study we included participants, who (i) had no history of COVID-19 and no detectable SARS CoV-2 anti-N IgG antibodies at enrollment (ii) had subsequently at least one documented positive SARS-CoV-2 polymerase chain reaction (PCR) test and (iii) had available blood samples within the time intervals of interest, namely pre-infection with SARS-CoV-2 (T1); 3–6 months post-infection (T2) and 12 months or longer post-infection (T3). All participants received at least one dose of COVID-19 vaccine (BnT162b2) prior to infection. HCW who fulfilled these conditions and responded to a questionnaire inquiring about post-acute COVID-19 infection symptoms (described below) were eligible to participate in the study. We excluded participants who reported in the questionnaire to have either hypertension, chronic kidney disease or used medications that might interfere with the RAAS. Participants who did not have blood specimens taken within the relevant time points were excluded as well.

sec.title: Questionnaire
sec.p: To determine whether participants experienced LC, we used a questionnaire based on the International Severe Acute Respiratory and emerging Infection Consortium (ISARIC) COVID 19 data collection tool [21, 22]. Participants were invited to complete the questionnaire by email or text messages that contained a link to an online survey. Those who did not reply were contacted via phone by the study team for follow-up. The questionnaire collected information about the dates of infection with SARS-CoV-2 and symptoms that appeared post-acute COVID-19 infection. Participants were asked to select symptoms from a predefined list and report their onset and end dates or indicate if the symptoms were still ongoing. In addition, information about high blood pressure, chronic kidney disease, and medications were collected to exclude patients with diseases or treatments that could interfere with the RAAS. Infection dates reported in the questionnaire were compared to PCR-positive records in the HCW cohort. In cases of discrepancy, the cohort records were considered more reliable and used as infection dates.

sec.title: Determination of participant LC status
sec.p: We determined LC status based on the criteria described by Soriano et al. [23]. Participants were considered LC positive if they had at least one LC-compatible symptom [4] that emerged within three months of disease onset, persisted for at least two months, and could not be explained by an alternative diagnosis. Symptoms that were considered are listed in supplementary table S1. Participants who did not report any symptoms were considered LC negative.

sec.title: ELISA assays
sec.p: Collected sera separated from whole blood were stored at -80 °C or -20 °C until analysis. Concentrations of sACE1, sACE2, Ang1-7 and AngII were measured in serum using commercially available enzyme linked immunosorbent assays (ELISAs): Human ACE ELISA kit (Elabscience, catalog no. E-EL-H6001), human ACE2 ELISA kit (Fine test, catalog no. EH0027), Human Ang1-7 ELISA kit (Elabscience, catalog no. E-EL-H5518), and Human ANG-II ELISA kit (Elabscience, catalog no. E-EL-H0326). Serum samples were thawed and analyzed in duplicates according to the manufacturer’s instructions. Preliminary tests were performed using a small number of serum samples to determine whether these kits could be used to detect RAAS components in our frozen serum samples and to determine suitable working dilutions (data not shown). For ACE1 analyses, serum samples were diluted x20. For ACE2 analyses, serum samples were diluted x2. Ang1-7 and AngII serum samples were not diluted. Optical density was read by Asys UVM 340 Microplate Reader (Biochrom) at 450 nm and concentrations were calculated using Arigo Biolaboratories’ GainData ELISA data analysis website [24].

sec.title: Data analysis
sec.p: We compared groups based on LC status (LC negative vs. LC positive). Gender distribution was analyzed using the Chi-squared test. Age distribution was reported as mean ± standard deviation (SD) and was compared using Student’s t-test. We determined the infection period according to the timing of dominant SARS-CoV-2 strains in Israel. Levels of the soluble RAAS components (ACE, ACE2, Ang 1–7, and Ang II) were reported as median and interquartile range (IQR) and were compared between groups at each time point (T1-T3) using Mann-Whitney U tests with Bonferroni’s correction applied for multiple comparisons. To produce a more realistic estimate of uncertainty while avoiding distributional assumptions, 95% confidence intervals (CIs) around medians were calculated using nonparametric bootstrapping with 10,000 resamples. Given the smaller sample sizes at later time points, bootstrapped CIs were interpreted cautiously due to instability from limited resampling variability. To assess differences in the levels of the four RAAS components within each group over time (T1 vs. T2 and T1 vs. T3), we used Wilcoxon signed-rank tests with Bonferroni’s correction applied for multiple comparisons. Additional subgroup analyses were performed after stratifying participants by sex (female, male) and by age group (19–40 years, 41–69 years). Medians and IQRs were reported, and comparisons were similarly performed using Mann-Whitney U tests with Bonferroni’s correction applied for multiple comparisons and bootstrapped 95% CIs calculated around medians using 10,000 resamples. All analyses were conducted using R statistical software (version 4.2.1), and data visualization was carried out with GraphPad.

sec.title: Participants
sec.p: Out of 489 participants with at least one documented positive PCR test, 108 had no detectable anti-N IgG, had available samples at the selected time points, and completed the questionnaire. After applying our exclusion criteria, 76 participants were included in the analysis, 38 of whom reported experiencing LC and 38 of whom did not (Fig. 1). This numerical balance occurred by chance and was not the result of any deliberate matching or selection. The majority of participants were female (n = 56, 73%), and the age and gender distributions were similar between the LC positive and LC negative groups (Table 1). Most participants in the LC positive group were infected prior to the Omicron-dominant period, while the LC negative group was predominantly infected during Omicron (Table 1). The most common LC symptoms reported by LC positive participants were neurological and neuromuscular (57.9%), systemic (55.2%), cardiopulmonary (52.6%), and musculoskeletal (42.1%) symptoms. Psychological, social, neurocognitive, and gastrointestinal symptoms were less common in our cohort, with a prevalence of less than 25%. None of our participants reported ongoing symptoms of upper respiratory origin (Supplementary Table S1).

We found no statistically significant differences in the levels of sACE, sACE2, and AngII) between participants with and without LC at any time point (Fig. 1; Table 2). These findings remained consistent when stratified by sex and by age group, with no significant differences observed in any of the age or sex sub-groups between those who reported Long COVID symptoms and those who didn’t in any component at any time point (Supplementary Table S4). While a nominally lower AngII level was observed among LC-negative participants at T1, this difference did not remain significant after correction for multiple testing (Bonferroni-adjusted P = 0.363), and no difference in AngII levels post-infection was observed between LC positive and LC negative participants. Additionally, bootstrapped 95% CIs around medians demonstrated significant overlap between groups for all biomarkers at all timepoints (Table 2, Supplementary Tables S2-S4). Within-group comparisons using Wilcoxon signed-rank tests corrected for multiple comparisons also revealed no significant changes over time in the levels of sACE, sACE2 and Ang II from T1 and T2 or from T1 and T3 (Bonferroni-adjusted P > 0.05 for all values; see Supplementary Tables S2 and S3 for component levels at each time point). While median Ang1–7 concentrations appeared higher at T2 compared to T1 in both groups, these differences did not remain significant after applying Bonferroni’s correction (P = 0.389 among LC positive participants; P = 0.174 among LC negative participants). Given the small sample size, skewed biomarker distributions, and potential for measurement and classification error in LC status, these analyses may be underpowered to detect small-to-moderate true differences. Accordingly, results should be interpreted as exploratory and hypothesis-generating rather than confirmatory.

caption.p: Levels of the four renin-angiotensin aldosterone system (RAAS) components (ACE, ACE 2, Ang1-7 and AngII) in the serum of participants who did not report long COVID (LC negative) and those who did (LC positive) at three time points: T1 (pre-infection), T2 (3–6 months post-infection), and T3 (12 months post-infection)

Comparison of four renin–angiotensin–aldosterone system (RAAS) components (ACE, ACE2, Ang1–7, and AngII) in serum samples from participants with and without long COVID across three time points: T1 (pre-infection), T2 (3–6 months post-infection), and T3 (12 months post-infection). Values are presented as medians with interquartile ranges (IQR), bootstrapped 95% confidence intervals (CIs), and P-values with bonferroni correction. NA: data not available

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: In this study, we compared the levels of four soluble RAAS components (sACE, sACE2, Ang1-7, and AngII) between infected individuals reporting LC symptoms and those without, within a cohort of healthcare workers at Ziv Medical Center in Safed, Israel, pre-infection, at 3–6 months and 12 + months post-infection. Given the central role of the ACE2 receptor in the pathogenesis of acute SARS-CoV-2 infection, and proposed mechanisms suggesting that the RAAS may contribute to LC development, we hypothesized that we could detect differences in the soluble levels of RAAS components between individuals with and without LC, potentially implicating this system in LC pathogenesis. Our findings do not support the hypothesis that differences in the soluble levels of RAAS components contribute to the development of LC within the time points measured in this study. However, these results should be interpreted cautiously, as small sample sizes and highly unstable biomarkers limit statistical power. Within the limits of this study design, we did not detect evidence of RAAS dysregulation associated with LC.

Both ACE and ACE2 are membrane-bound enzymes expressed in various tissues. Following cleavage from the cell surface, active soluble forms of those enzymes are released into circulation [25, 26]. Consistent with our findings, another study reported no significant difference in sACE levels between COVID-19 patients and healthy controls, both during acute illness and four months later [17], although the study was not specifically focused on LC patients. An increase in sACE2 levels was observed in hospitalized COVID-19 patients during their hospital admission, likely due to upregulated ACE2 expression and increased ectodomain shedding. However, this increase was transient, with sACE2 levels returning to baseline four months after infection [17]. In our study, we also observed a transient rise in Ang1-7 levels at 3–6 months post-infection in both LC positive and LC negative participants, consistent with previous studies [27]. However, because this increase was seen in both groups, it does not appear to be specific to LC pathogenesis. Additionally, patients reporting LC symptoms were more likely to have been infected during the Omicron-dominant period (the “Omicron wave”), compared to those not reporting LC. This aligns with previous evidence suggesting that LC is less likely following infection with Omicron compared to infection with Delta or pre-Delta variants [28–30].

This study has several limitations. One major limitation is the small sample size, restricted by the number of individuals in our original cohort who met eligibility criteria. This limitation limits the interpretative power of our results, namely whether the lack of difference is genuine or due to a lack of statistical power to detect small changes. While we cannot provide a definite answer, the comparable point estimates between the two groups at most time points suggest the reported differences may not be entirely due to underpowering.

Furthermore, measurement of circulating RAAS peptides is technically challenging due to their short half-lives and susceptibility to degradation during sample processing. Ang1-7 has a half-life of 30 min, while AngII has an even shorter half-life of only 15 s [31, 32]. It is possible that the ELISA method we used to detect RAAS components in frozen serum samples may have limited sensitivity compared to more advanced techniques, such as LC-MS/MS, as used by Reindl et al. [27]. Moreover, circulating RAAS components may not fully represent the status of tissue RAAS (local system), which could be dysregulated despite the comparable levels of the studied components in the serum of both groups.

Nevertheless, we were able to detect all the components in the serum, and it is reasonable to assume that any dysregulation likely to cause clinical symptoms would have been reflected in the differences in levels between our two groups. It is important to note that we did not purport to interpret the biological significance of the levels of circulating RAAS components but rather to detect significant differences in patients who reported LC-compatible symptoms and those who did not.

Our classification of LC relied on participant self-report of symptoms and their durations, introducing potential misclassification bias. Although SARS-CoV-2 infection dates were validated using cohort records, recall bias and/or symptom misattribution remains possible. While some participants had difficulty recalling the symptoms they experienced or the exact duration of those symptoms, it is unlikely that participants would forget long-lasting symptoms, particularly given the widespread nature and coverage of the COVID-19 pandemic at the time. We also could not account for the impact of SARS-CoV-2 reinfection. Only 5 of the 76 participants (2 controls and 3 LC cases) reported multiple infections during the study period, which was insufficient to support sub-group analysis.

Participants in the LC-negative group were more likely to have been infected later in the pandemic, as suggested by the higher proportion of Omicron infections. Some participants categorized as LC-negative could have developed symptoms later potentially resulting in misclassification bias, however all participants were assessed at least six months post-infection, a period after which the onset of LC symptoms is uncommon, so it is unlikely participants would’ve gone on to develop LC. It is also possible that the temporal distribution of infections may have further attenuated group differences, as LC appears less common after Omicron infection [28–30]. Participants who reported at least one ongoing symptom after the acute phase of COVID-19, as reviewed by Michelen et al. [33], were classified as LC positive. However, participants classified as LC-positive may have reported non-specific symptoms unrelated to infection, resulting in misclassification and potentially biasing the results toward the null. To mitigate this risk, we ensured that (a) there was no alternative explanation for symptoms and (b) the onset of symptoms occurred after infection. Using more specific criteria, such as symptom clusters, would have further reduced the risk of misclassification, but this would have required a larger sample size than the limited number of participants available in our study.

Additionally, none of the participants were hospitalized during the acute phase of COVID-19, which limits generalizability to populations with more severe acute illness. Since the likelihood and severity of LC are associated with the severity of the acute illness, our cohort may underestimate possible associations between RAAS disruption and LC.

Taken together, our findings suggest no clear evidence of persistent RAAS dysregulation in this cohort, but definitive conclusions cannot be drawn given the methodological and statistical limitations. Larger, longitudinal studies that include participants across the full spectrum of acute disease severity, use standardized LC definitions, and employ sensitive assays for intracellular RAAS components are needed to further clarify if and how dysregulation of the RAAS system contributes to LC pathophysiology.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p: Below is the link to the electronic supplementary material.

fn.p: Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.

notes.title: Author contributions
notes.p: TLT analyzed the specimens and contributed to writing the first draft; NT conducted the data analysis with TB and contributed to writing the first draft; SL coordinated the writing of the first draft and contributed to its writing; YF collected and prepared the specimens; TB conducted the data analysis with NT; ZA provided guidance on laboratory testing and expert knowledge on the RAAS; ME and KAJ designed the study, oversaw its execution. ME, KAJ and ZA interpreted the data.

notes.title: Funding
notes.p: The study was funded by and Israel Science foundation grant, number 2396/23.

notes.title: Data availability
notes.p: The dataset used in this analysis is available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

notes.title: Ethics approval and consent to participate
notes.p: The study was performed in accordance with the ethical standards as laid down in the 1964 Declaration of Helsinki. The study was approved by the Ethics committee of Ziv Medical Center. The study received approval and consent from Ziv Medical Center Helsinki committee, number ZIV-0014–22. (0133-20-ZIV and 07-21-ZIV). All participants provided informed consent.

notes.title: Competing interests
notes.p: The authors declare no competing interests.
